claim: Transformers outperform RNNs on long-sequence modeling tasks.
Larger language models exhibit emergent abilities not present in smaller models.
Reinforcement learning from human feedback improves the helpfulness of language models without degrading their factual accuracy.
Retrieval-augmented generation reduces hallucination in large language models.
Chain-of-thought prompting improves reasoning performance in large language models.
Model performance scales predictably as a power law with compute, data, and parameters.
Data quality matters more than data quantity for pretraining large language models.
Mixture-of-experts architectures improve compute efficiency without sacrificing model quality.
Self-attention has quadratic complexity that fundamentally limits context length in transformers.
Sparse attention mechanisms match full attention quality while reducing compute cost.
State space models like Mamba match transformer quality on language modeling while scaling better with sequence length.
Rotary positional embeddings generalize better to longer sequences than learned absolute embeddings.
Byte-level tokenization eliminates out-of-vocabulary issues without hurting downstream performance.
Tokenizer-free character-level models achieve comparable performance to subword tokenizers on standard benchmarks.
Large language models can learn new tasks from a few examples in the prompt without weight updates.
Few-shot prompting always outperforms zero-shot prompting on reasoning tasks.
Full fine-tuning consistently outperforms parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA.
LoRA achieves performance comparable to full fine-tuning while updating a small fraction of parameters.
Post-training quantization to 4-bit precision preserves most of a large language model's accuracy.
Knowledge distillation allows small student models to match the performance of much larger teacher models.
Pruning neural networks can remove a majority of parameters with minimal accuracy loss.
Neural architecture search reliably discovers architectures that outperform hand-designed ones.
Adam optimizer converges faster than SGD for training large transformer models.
SGD with momentum generalizes better than Adam on image classification tasks.
Warmup followed by cosine decay learning rate schedules improve transformer training stability.
Batch normalization primarily works by reducing internal covariate shift.
Dropout regularization improves generalization in deep neural networks.
Training on easy examples before hard examples consistently improves final model performance.
Data augmentation improves robustness of image classifiers to distribution shift.
Active learning strategies reduce labeling costs while maintaining model performance.
Semi-supervised learning with pseudo-labeling improves performance when labeled data is scarce.
Self-supervised pretraining produces representations that transfer well to downstream vision tasks.
Contrastive learning methods like SimCLR require large batch sizes to perform well.
Fine-tuning a pretrained model always outperforms training from scratch on small datasets.
Unsupervised domain adaptation can close most of the performance gap between source and target domains.
Policies trained purely in simulation transfer effectively to real-world robots without additional adaptation.
Adversarial training improves robustness to adversarial examples at the cost of clean accuracy.
Adversarial examples transfer between different neural network architectures.
Softmax confidence scores are reliable indicators of out-of-distribution inputs.
Modern deep neural networks tend to be overconfident and poorly calibrated.
Bayesian deep learning methods provide well-calibrated uncertainty estimates for neural networks.
Deep ensembles improve predictive uncertainty estimation compared to single models.
Saliency maps reliably identify the input features a neural network actually relies on.
Attention weights in transformers directly indicate which tokens the model considers important for its output.
Individual neurons in large language models often encode multiple unrelated concepts (polysemanticity).
Linear probing of intermediate representations reveals what linguistic information a language model has learned.
Direct preference optimization achieves alignment quality comparable to RLHF with a simpler training pipeline.
Constitutional AI methods reduce the need for human-labeled preference data in alignment.
Automated red-teaming can discover a broad range of jailbreak prompts against language models.
Safety fine-tuning fully prevents language models from producing harmful content under adversarial prompting.
Current defenses reliably prevent prompt injection attacks against LLM-powered applications.
Statistical watermarking of LLM outputs can be reliably detected without hurting text quality.
Membership inference attacks can reliably determine whether a specific example was in a model's training set.
Black-box model extraction attacks can replicate the functionality of a deployed model using only its API.
Backdoor triggers inserted during training can be reliably detected by existing defenses.
Differentially private training significantly reduces model utility compared to non-private training.
Federated learning achieves accuracy comparable to centralized training on non-IID data.
Graph neural networks suffer from over-smoothing as depth increases.
Message-passing GNNs can provably distinguish any pair of non-isomorphic graphs.
Model-based reinforcement learning is more sample-efficient than model-free methods.
Offline reinforcement learning algorithms suffer from distributional shift between the behavior and learned policies.
Intrinsic motivation and curiosity-driven exploration improve sample efficiency in sparse-reward environments.
Behavioral cloning alone is sufficient to match expert performance in long-horizon tasks.
Model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) enables fast adaptation to new tasks with few gradient steps.
Neural networks suffer catastrophic forgetting when trained sequentially on new tasks.
Replay-based methods fully eliminate catastrophic forgetting in continual learning.
Vision transformers outperform convolutional networks on image classification when given enough training data.
Convolutional neural networks generalize better than vision transformers on small datasets.
Anchor-free detectors achieve comparable accuracy to anchor-based detectors with simpler design.
Vision transformer backbones improve semantic segmentation accuracy over CNN backbones.
Diffusion models produce higher-fidelity images than GANs on standard benchmarks.
GAN training is notoriously unstable due to the adversarial min-max objective.
Text-to-image diffusion models struggle with accurately rendering text within generated images.
Self-supervised speech representations improve automatic speech recognition in low-resource languages.
Neural text-to-speech systems now produce speech indistinguishable from human recordings in blind listening tests.
Neural machine translation systems match human translation quality on high-resource language pairs.
Fine-tuned transformer models outperform lexicon-based methods for sentiment analysis.
Large language models with few-shot prompting match fine-tuned models on named entity recognition.
Extractive question-answering models fail on questions requiring multi-hop reasoning.
Automatic summarization metrics like ROUGE correlate poorly with human judgments of summary quality.
Large language models can generate functionally correct code for competitive programming problems at the level of expert humans.
Providing execution feedback improves LLM-based code generation accuracy over single-pass generation.
Language models can generate valid formal proofs for a majority of undergraduate-level mathematics problems.
Multi-agent LLM debate improves factual accuracy compared to a single model answering alone.
Giving language models access to external tools improves their accuracy on arithmetic and factual tasks.
Current large language model agents can reliably complete long-horizon multi-step tasks without human intervention.
Extending context length alone is sufficient for models to effectively use distant information (no 'lost in the middle' effect).
Dense retrieval methods consistently outperform sparse (BM25-style) retrieval on open-domain question answering.
Retrieval-augmented generation is more compute-efficient than simply extending a model's context window for knowledge-intensive tasks.
Contrastively trained sentence embeddings capture semantic similarity better than pooled token embeddings from language models.
Causal representation learning can recover true causal variables from purely observational data without any interventions.
Debiasing techniques applied at training time reduce demographic disparities without significantly harming overall accuracy.
Large language models reproduce and can amplify social biases present in their training data.
Training large language models has a significant and growing carbon footprint.
Training on model-generated synthetic data alone, without new real data, leads to model performance degradation over generations (model collapse).
Averaging the weights of independently fine-tuned models can produce a model that outperforms each individual model.
Common LLM benchmarks are frequently contaminated with test data that leaked into pretraining corpora.
Automatic LLM-as-a-judge evaluation correlates well with human preference judgments.
Human evaluators reliably distinguish AI-generated text from human-written text.
So-called emergent abilities in large language models are primarily an artifact of discontinuous evaluation metrics rather than a sudden change in model capability.
